# Last Time

::: {.incremental}
+ **Pseudodynamic Memory**
+ **Dynamic Memory and Dynamic Arrays**
:::

::: notes
Recall capacity versus logical size, runtime allocation, ownership, cleanup, and allocate-copy-release-transfer resizing.
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

# Today's Agenda

- Structures
- Arrays of Structures
- Binary Records
- Abstract Data Types — Part 1

::: notes

:::

<!-- Slide 2 -->

{{< include topic_1_structures.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_2_arrays_of_structures.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_3_binary_records.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_4_abstract_data_types.qmd >}}

---

# Key Takeaways

- Structures define complete record types from related members.
- Arrays of structures keep each record intact through searching, sorting, storage, and resizing.
- Fixed-size binary records enable direct access but depend on a controlled file layout.
- An ADT is defined by values, operations, and contracts rather than one representation.

<!-- Slide 136 -->

---

# Next Time

- Abstract Data Types — Part 2
- Object-Oriented Programming
- Introduction to Classes

<!-- Slide 137 -->

---

# Appendix

<!-- Slide 138 -->

---

## Structures: Good Practices

- Define a structure before prototypes that use it.
- Give types noun names and members clear domain names.
- Use default member initializers for safe starting values.
- Pass read-only records by `const` reference.

<!-- Slide 139 -->

---

## Structures: Common Mistakes

- Omitting the semicolon after the closing brace
- Writing `Employee.name` instead of `worker.name`
- Assuming aggregate values map by name rather than declaration order
- Passing a large read-only record by value accidentally

<!-- Slide 140 -->

---

## Structures: More Examples

```cpp
struct Point { double x = 0.0; double y = 0.0; };
struct Date { int month = 1; int day = 1; int year = 2000; };
struct Book { string title; string author; double price = 0.0; };
```

<!-- Slide 141 -->

---

## Structures: Practice Problems

1. Define a `Product` structure with an ID, description, price, and quantity.
2. Write a function that returns the inventory value of one product.
3. Add a nested `Date` member representing the last restock date.
4. Compare the sizes of two equivalent structures with different member orders.

<!-- Slide 142 -->

---

## Structures: AI Search Terms

- C++ aggregate initialization
- C++ default member initializer struct
- C++ struct padding alignment
- C++ pointer to struct arrow operator
- C++ pass struct by const reference

<!-- Slide 143 -->

---

## Structures: Questions for Reflection

- When should several variables become one record?
- Which members determine whether two records represent the same entity?
- Why can member order affect both initialization and memory layout?

<!-- Slide 144 -->

---

## Arrays of Structures: Good Practices

- Keep capacity and logical size as separate values.
- Search by stable identifiers rather than display names.
- Swap or shift complete records instead of coordinating individual members.
- Use `const Employee&` in range-based loops that only read.

<!-- Slide 145 -->

---

## Arrays of Structures: Common Mistakes

- Writing `staff.name[i]` instead of `staff[i].name`
- Traversing to capacity when only `count` records are valid
- Tracking a maximum value but losing the record index
- Updating `count` before confirming an append or removal succeeded

<!-- Slide 146 -->

---

## Arrays of Structures: More Examples

```cpp
struct City {
    string name;
    int population;
};

City cities[] {
    {"Sacramento", 526384},
    {"Roseville", 154817}
};
```

<!-- Slide 147 -->

---

## Arrays of Structures: Practice Problems

1. Find the least expensive product in an inventory array.
2. Sort students by grade while keeping names attached.
3. Remove a record by ID while preserving a packed valid range.
4. Resize a dynamic array of records and append one new record.

<!-- Slide 148 -->

---

## Arrays of Structures: AI Search Terms

- C++ array of structs
- C++ search array of records
- C++ sort structs by member
- C++ logical size versus capacity
- C++ range for const reference struct

<!-- Slide 149 -->

---

## Arrays of Structures: Questions for Reflection

- Why is an index often more useful than a copied search result?
- What invariant does a packed pseudodynamic record array maintain?
- When would `vector<Employee>` be preferable to a dynamic array?

<!-- Slide 150 -->

---

## Binary Records: Good Practices

- Use fixed-size, trivially copyable disk-record types.
- Validate indexes before calculating offsets.
- Check every read and write operation.
- Store a format version when records must survive program changes.

<!-- Slide 151 -->

---

## Binary Records: Common Mistakes

- Writing `string` objects or pointers as raw bytes
- Confusing human record numbers with zero-based indexes
- Seeking while the stream remains in a failed state
- Assuming binary files are encrypted or portable

<!-- Slide 152 -->

---

## Binary Records: More Examples

```cpp
struct SensorRecord {
    int sensorID;
    long timestamp;
    double reading;
    bool active;
};

static_assert(is_trivially_copyable<SensorRecord>::value,
              "Raw record required");
```

<!-- Slide 153 -->

---

## Binary Records: Practice Problems

1. Calculate the byte offset for record index 12.
2. Write a function that reports the number of complete records in a file.
3. Update one sensor reading in place.
4. Design a tombstone strategy and describe how deleted slots are reused.

<!-- Slide 154 -->

---

## Binary Records: AI Search Terms

- C++ binary file fixed size records
- C++ seekg seekp record offset
- C++ trivially copyable serialization
- C++ struct padding binary file
- binary file format versioning

<!-- Slide 155 -->

---

## Binary Records: Questions for Reflection

- Which assumptions make raw record access possible?
- Why can a harmless source-code change invalidate an old binary file?
- When should a program use a defined portable format instead?

<!-- Slide 156 -->

---

## Abstract Data Types — Part 1: Good Practices

- Define the values and operations before choosing representation.
- State invariants and failure behavior explicitly.
- Keep client code dependent on operations rather than members.
- Treat representation choices as replaceable decisions.

<!-- Slide 157 -->

---

## Abstract Data Types — Part 1: Common Mistakes

- Treating ADT as another name for `class`
- Confusing a data structure with the behavior it implements
- Omitting failure behavior from an operation’s contract
- Calling members private without identifying the invariant being protected

<!-- Slide 158 -->

---

## Abstract Data Types — Part 1: More Examples

| ADT | Possible Operations | Possible Representation |
|---|---|---|
| Queue | enqueue, dequeue, front | Array or vector |
| Set | insert, erase, contains | Array or tree |
| Date | advance, compare, format | Three integers or day count |

<!-- Slide 159 -->

---

## Abstract Data Types — Part 1: Practice Problems

1. Specify the values and operations of a `BankAccount` ADT.
2. State an invariant for a bounded stack.
3. Write preconditions and postconditions for `withdraw`.
4. Propose two representations for a date and compare their tradeoffs.

<!-- Slide 160 -->

---

## Abstract Data Types — Part 1: AI Search Terms

- abstract data type values operations
- ADT versus data structure
- representation invariant programming
- precondition postcondition contract
- abstraction versus encapsulation

<!-- Slide 161 -->

---

## Abstract Data Types — Part 1: Questions for Reflection

- What should clients know about a type, and what should remain replaceable?
- Why does observable behavior matter more than source-code secrecy?
- Which limitation of public structures will classes address next?

<!-- Slide 162 -->
